CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized (intro → methods → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early, including the automated evaluation methodology, the repetitive-text penalty, and the released corpus. Notation and metrics are consistently defined and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables/examples effectively illustrate comparisons between LSTM and n-gram baselines. From a theory-oriented perspective I would have liked a clearer formal problem statement framing "ghostwriting" and the evaluation objectives, but overall the writing is good and free of major ambiguities or grammatical issues. | B(4): The paper appears well-organized (intro → methods → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early—an evaluation methodology, a fully automated penalized similarity measure, and a released corpus for 13 artists—which aligns well with my emphasis on dataset and reproducibility. Notation and hyperparameter reporting are adequate for reproducibility, and writing quality is described as good; figures/tables and examples seem informative for the empirical claims. Minor presentation weaknesses remain around the exact operationalization of the automatic repetition penalty and some preprocessing heuristics (possible inclusion of non-verse text), which could use clearer exposition. Overall the paper is clear and practical, with only small areas that would benefit from expanded detail.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized (intro → methods → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early, including the automated evaluation methodology, the repetitive-text penalty, and the released corpus. Notation and metrics are consistently defined and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables/examples effectively illustrate comparisons between LSTM and n-gram baselines. From a theory-oriented perspective I would have liked a clearer formal problem statement framing "ghostwriting" and the evaluation objectives, but overall the writing is good and free of major ambiguities or grammatical issues.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper appears well-organized (intro → methods → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early—an evaluation methodology, a fully automated penalized similarity measure, and a released corpus for 13 artists—which aligns well with my emphasis on dataset and reproducibility. Notation and hyperparameter reporting are adequate for reproducibility, and writing quality is described as good; figures/tables and examples seem informative for the empirical claims. Minor presentation weaknesses remain around the exact operationalization of the automatic repetition penalty and some preprocessing heuristics (possible inclusion of non-verse text), which could use clearer exposition. Overall the paper is clear and practical, with only small areas that would benefit from expanded detail.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper lacks fundamental algorithmic novelty and mainly applies established metrics (rhyme density, max similarity) to generation models, but they also concur that automating a previously semi-automatic evaluation and releasing a 13-artist annotated corpus are useful practical contributions. Given the work is an applied, non-trivial extension that aids reproducibility and evaluation rather than introducing a new theoretical or methodological paradigm, a middle-ground score reflects its solid engineering/dataset value without overstating originality.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper primarily automates an existing semi-automatic evaluation (Potash et al., 2015) by adding a repetition penalty and pairs that with standard LSTM vs n-gram comparisons; this is an engineering refinement rather than a new theoretical or algorithmic contribution. The release of a 13-artist corpus and replication for one artist (Fabolous) are useful resources but do not constitute a novel method. There is no formal problem reformulation or new technique beyond applying established metrics (rhyme density, max similarity) and an ad-hoc penalty, so an expert seeking conceptual or algorithmic innovation would not be surprised.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper offers a notable, practical novelty by fully automating a previously semi-automatic evaluation (Potash et al., 2015) with a defensible repetition penalty and by releasing a 13-artist lyrics corpus annotated for stylistic similarity — contributions I value as an evaluation-focused practitioner. Techniques for generation (LSTM vs n-gram) are standard, and rhyme-density/max-similarity metrics build on prior work, so the methodological advance is an applied, non-trivial extension rather than a wholly new algorithmic paradigm. The authors clearly differentiate their work from prior methods and provide empirical validation that the automated protocol reproduces earlier results, making the contribution informative to experts focused on evaluation methodology and dataset resources.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes a clear, practical contribution by releasing a 13-artist corpus and a fully automatic, reproducible evaluation pipeline that will be useful to researchers in stylistic/creative text generation. The work’s impact is somewhat scoped—limited theoretical novelty, narrow baselines, and preprocessing caveats—but its artifact and validated evaluation procedure are likely to influence benchmarking and follow-up work in creative language evaluation, so a slightly above-average impact score is warranted.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a concrete evaluation bottleneck in creative language generation and provides a useful artifact (a 13-artist lyric corpus) plus a fully automated variant of a prior semi-automatic evaluation—practical contributions that practitioners can reuse. However, from a theory-first perspective its impact is moderated: there is no formal problem definition or theoretical analysis of the automated metric, baselines are narrow (only n-grams vs LSTMs), and the work largely presents empirical replication and engineering (automatic repetition penalty) rather than new algorithms or analytical insight. These factors make the contribution practically useful but unlikely to change fundamental thinking or open major new research directions beyond the subfield.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses an important and understudied problem—evaluating creative language generation for rap ghostwriting—by releasing a 13-artist lyrics corpus with line-level stylistic annotations and by operationalizing a fully automatic evaluation that improves on a previous semi-automatic procedure (automatic repetition penalty + rhyme-density and similarity regression). From an evaluation-practicality perspective this is likely to change how researchers benchmark stylistic generation: the combination of public data, validated manual annotations, and a defensible automated pipeline makes the contribution readily reusable and reproducible. While its impact is somewhat scoped to creative-text evaluation and has limits (vocabulary/tone assessment and preprocessing caveats), it opens clear directions for follow-up work and wider adoption in stylistic generation research.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses a relevant applied problem, releases a useful dataset, and provides a reproducible mixed human/automated evaluation protocol. I rate it a 4 because while the contribution is strong in dataset and evaluation practice (important for the community), it lacks deeper theoretical framing and stronger contemporary baselines, limiting its methodological novelty for a top-tier venue.

reviewer_a.justification: Borderline appropriate (3) — the paper tackles an interesting applied problem in creative language generation and provides a released dataset and a mixed manual/automated evaluation protocol, which are relevant to the NLP community. However, from a theory-first perspective it lacks a formal problem statement or theoretical analysis of the proposed automated metric, and the empirical novelty is mainly automating a prior semi-automatic procedure rather than introducing new algorithms or guarantees. The evaluation uses sensible human and automatic measures but compares only against n-gram baselines (LSTM is included but no stronger contemporary neural baselines or diagnostic theoretical analysis), which weakens its appeal for a top-tier venue focused on algorithmic or theoretical contribution.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP venue: it tackles creative language generation (rap ghostwriting), releases a new corpus for 13 artists, and advances evaluation methodology by fully automating a prior semi-automatic procedure while adding a defensible repetition penalty. The work combines line-level human annotations (fluency/coherence and page-level style matching) with validated automated metrics (rhyme density, max similarity, Match%/MatchA%), matching the empirical rigor and reproducibility standards I prioritize. The evaluation design, dataset release, and empirical comparisons between LSTM and n-gram baselines target the community that cares about robust, reproducible evaluation of creative generation, so scope and audience are well aligned.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes clear empirical claims, provides human annotations, a released corpus, and a reasonable automated metric that is empirically validated (including a replication). They diverge on the sufficiency of evaluation: Reviewer A rightly points out weaker baselines and limited theoretical justification for some automated choices, while Reviewer B views the methodology as largely sound with only modest gaps. Given that the results are supportive but methodological and comparative weaknesses limit confidence in the soundness of the technical claims, a score of 3 is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents clear empirical claims and supports them with experiments (manual annotations, rhyme-density and similarity metrics) and a released corpus, but offers no formal problem statement or theoretical analysis of the proposed automated evaluation—only empirical validation. The experimental methodology uses reasonable metrics but compares the LSTM mainly to n-gram baselines (n=1..9), failing my requirement for multiple, diverse and strong baselines (e.g., recent neural baselines), which weakens claims of superiority. The automated penalty for repetition and the regression linking maximum similarity to authorship scores are plausible but not justified theoretically and may introduce unsupported assumptions. Overall the conclusions are partially justified by the presented results, but methodological and theoretical gaps reduce confidence in the technical contribution.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and largely supported: it combines line-level human annotations with a defensible automated metric (rhyme density + max-similarity with a repetition penalty), releases a 13-artist corpus, and empirically shows LSTMs outperform n-gram baselines while replicating prior semi-automatic results for Fabolous. The experimental methodology appears sound (artist-specific models, reasonable baselines, human evaluation design) and hyperparameters are reported, but the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or broader validation of the automated procedure beyond the Fabolous replication—minor gaps that do not fundamentally undermine the conclusions. Overall the technical work is rigorous and reproducible in spirit, with only modest evidence gaps that merit clarification rather than indicating serious flaws.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes a useful, reproducible empirical contribution (dataset, hyperparameters, manual annotations) and discusses limitations, but lacks broader modern baselines and thorough ablation/sensitivity analyses—particularly around the automatic repetition penalty and code release. Given the solid experimental coverage across 13 artists and practical value, but acknowledging the gaps in diagnostic analysis and replication details, a score of 4 reflects a strong and useful paper that is not yet fully comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a focused empirical study with a released dataset and hyperparameters, and it documents limitations and reproduci-bility-relevant artifacts (dataset, hyperparams) — sufficient to support many of its claims. However, from the summary I see only an LSTM vs n-gram comparison (no other modern neural baselines or stylistic models), and there is little evidence of systematic ablations or sensitivity analyses beyond the single automatic repetition penalty; these gaps weaken causal claims about what causes improved style matching. Related work is treated adequately and failure modes are discussed, but the experimental breadth and diagnostic/theoretical analysis expected by my standards are missing, so the work is competent but not comprehensive.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a solid, practically-oriented evaluation: manual line-level annotations (fluency, coherence, style matching) plus an automated pipeline that fully automates a prior semi-automatic method by adding a defensible repetition penalty, and it evaluates across 13 artists with LSTM vs n-gram baselines—supporting the main claims. It also releases a lyrics corpus and reports hyperparameters, and discusses limitations (vocabulary, preprocessing heuristics), which strengthens reproducibility and critical appraisal. Missing, however, are clear ablation or sensitivity analyses around the new automatic penalty and no explicit mention of released code, leaving some gaps for exact replication and understanding of parameter robustness. Overall the experimental coverage is strong but not exhaustively analyzed, so I rate it a 4.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes fair and informative comparisons by evaluating LSTMs against a wide range of n-gram baselines and by reproducing prior semi-automatic results, which validates the evaluation setup. However, they also agree the comparison set is not fully comprehensive—missing modern neural/transformer baselines and some deeper analysis or code release—so while the empirical comparison is solid and appropriate for the task, it is not exhaustive. I therefore assign a slightly positive but not perfect score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates its LSTM ghostwriter against a range of n-gram baselines on the same corpus and metrics, and explicitly reproduces and situates results relative to prior semi-automatic work (Potash et al., 2015), so comparisons are fair and prior performance is acknowledged. However, comparisons are limited: the only baselines are n-gram models (n=1..9) and no recent neural or stronger sequence models are included, which violates my expectation for diverse, modern baselines. The authors provide some analysis of LSTM failure modes (vocabulary integration) but do not deeply analyze why their model outperforms n-grams, so the empirical comparisons are informative but incomplete.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares LSTM models against a range of n-gram baselines (n=1..9) and validates an automated evaluation by replicating prior semi-automatic results (Potash et al., 2015) on Fabolous, which shows good and appropriate baseline selection for the task. Comparisons appear fair (same corpus of 13 artists, same metrics like rhyme density and Match%), and the authors acknowledge prior results and analyze where LSTMs improve (generalization) and where they fall short (integrating new vocabulary). Some omissions remain—no mention of other modern neural or transformer baselines and code release is unclear—so while comparisons are solid and well-analyzed, they are not fully comprehensive.

main_claims: A comprehensive evaluation methodology for rap lyric ghostwriting combining manual (fluency, coherence, style matching) and automated metrics.
An improvement to a previously semi-automatic evaluation (Potash et al., 2015) that fully automates the procedure by penalizing repetitive text.
A release of a corpus of lyrics for 13 rap artists annotated for stylistic similarity, and demonstration that the automated methodology replicates prior semi-automatic results (for Fabolous).
Empirical illustration that an LSTM-based ghostwriter generalizes better than an n-gram baseline, while also identifying limitations of LSTMs (e.g., integrating new vocabulary).
_summary.methodology: Train artist-specific LSTM models to generate verses and evaluate them using (1) manual annotations at the line level for fluency, coherence, and page-level stylematching by human annotators, and (2) an automated evaluation combining rhyme density (Hirjee and Brown, 2010a) and a maximum similarity score regression—with an added automatic penalty for repetitive text; compare to n-gram baselines.
datasets: Corpus of lyrics for 13 rap artists collected from The Original Hip-Hop (Rap) Lyrics Archive (OHHLA.com), preprocessed and filtered (verses >=20 tokens), publicly released by the authors
limitations: Automated evaluation cannot fully assess vocabulary, tone, and themes preferred by an artist.
Preprocessing was heuristic and may include non-verse text (dialogue or chorus lines).
LSTM models have difficulty integrating new vocabulary, limiting desirable similarity scores.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - creative language generation (rap lyric ghostwriting)

reviewer_a.name: Theory-First Skeptic
reviewer_a.background: Senior researcher in computational linguistics with a focus on formal models of language and theoretical guarantees for generation algorithms.
traits: Demands a clear formal problem statement or theoretical contribution; deducts points if the paper presents only empirical comparisons without a formal definition or proven properties.
Penalizes papers that add incremental engineering to prior work without introducing new algorithms or analyses; subtracts score if the main novelty is automation of an existing procedure.
Requires at least three, diverse and strong baselines (including recent neural baselines); downgrades if baselines are weak (e.g., only n-gram plus one simple neural model).
Insists on diagnostic analyses that explain model behavior (e.g., ablations or theoretical insight into failure modes); penalizes purely empirical observations without mechanistic explanation.
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Prioritizes formal clarity and algorithmic novelty over dataset or evaluation engineering; skeptical of papers whose main claim is improved evaluation or replication without theoretical insight.

reviewer_b.name: Applied-Evals Enthusiast
reviewer_b.background: Experienced NLP practitioner and conference reviewer focused on evaluation methodology, dataset creation, and reproducible empirical work for creative language tasks.
traits: Strongly rewards release of new, well-annotated datasets and reproducibility artifacts; adds positive score if data and code are publicly released.

Values robust human evaluation design (e.g., line-level annotations, inter-annotator agreement) and practical automated metrics that correlate with human judgments; boosts score for thorough annotation protocols and validation.

Rewards papers that operationalize and fully automate prior semi-automatic procedures, especially when the automation includes defensible heuristics (e.g., penalties for repetition) and empirical validation.
Prefers applied contributions that advance evaluation practice or benchmarking, and is forgiving of limited theoretical novelty when the practical utility and empirical rigor are high.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Champions empirical rigor, dataset releases, and practical improvements to evaluation methodology, even if algorithmic novelty is modest.
